definitions.technical: A binary tree data structure in which every leaf node contains the hash of a data block and every non leaf node contains the hash of its two children. The single root hash (Merkle root) enables efficient verification that any specific transaction is included in a block without downloading the entire block. Bitcoin and Ethereum both store transaction Merkle roots in their block headers.
definitions.lay: A Merkle tree is a way of organizing data so that you can quickly verify whether a specific transaction is included in a block. It works by repeatedly combining pairs of data fingerprints until a single master fingerprint remains. Checking one transaction requires only a small number of steps rather than reviewing every transaction in the block.
definitions.parenthetical: (a hash based tree structure enabling efficient verification of data inclusion within a block)
definitions.legal_significance: Merkle proofs allow lightweight clients to verify transaction inclusion without full node operation, which matters for evidentiary standards. A party can demonstrate that a specific transaction occurred at a specific time by presenting a compact Merkle proof rather than the entire blockchain, reducing the burden of producing electronic records in discovery.
